players.name: Alexander Schlager
players.bio: Salzburg-born shot-stopper who guards the sticks for his hometown club. Schlager has been a dependable presence between the posts for Austria, earning 26 caps with calm authority. A hometown hero heading to his first World Cup.
players.facts: Born in Salzburg and plays for Salzburg — his commute to training may be the shortest of any World Cup keeper.

players.name: David Affengruber
players.bio: A rugged centre-back who made his international debut with just 1 cap to his name — talk about jumping in at the deep end. Affengruber plies his trade in Spain with Elche, bringing Bundesliga-forged steel to Austrian defending.
players.facts: With just 1 cap before this tournament, Affengruber is Austria's least-capped outfield player at the 2026 World Cup.

players.name: Kevin Danso
players.bio: Born in Voitsberg but boasting a Ghanaian father, Danso is a powerhouse centre-back who joined Tottenham Hotspur in a major move. Commanding in the air and fierce in the tackle, he's become one of Austria's defensive cornerstones.
players.facts: Danso was the subject of a collapsed transfer to Liverpool — one of the most dramatic deadline-day sagas in recent memory.

players.name: Xaver Schlager
players.bio: The engine room of the Austrian midfield, Schlager is a relentless box-to-box dynamo at RB Leipzig. Technically gifted and tactically intelligent, he's suffered serious injury setbacks but keeps bouncing back stronger.
players.facts: No relation to goalkeeper Alexander Schlager — Austria just really likes the surname. Two Schlagers, zero relation.

players.name: Stefan Posch
players.bio: A centre-back who moonlights as a set-piece threat, Posch has 5 international goals — remarkable for a defender. Cool under pressure and experienced in German football, he's a versatile cornerstone of Austria's backline.
players.facts: With 5 international goals from defence, Posch outscores several of Austria's forwards — defenders, who knew?

players.name: Nicolas Seiwald
players.bio: A product of Red Bull Salzburg's acclaimed academy, Seiwald has become Austria's midfield metronome. The RB Leipzig enforcer screens the defence with tireless pressing and intelligent positioning, all at just 25 years old.
players.facts: Seiwald hails from Kuchl — a village so small, it might have fewer residents than his Instagram followers.

players.name: Marko Arnautović
players.bio: Austria's all-time top scorer with 47 goals in 133 caps — a living legend. The mercurial striker has graced clubs across Europe including Inter Milan and Manchester United. At 37, this World Cup may be his final bow.
players.facts: Arnautović once received a 3-match ban for abusive language at Euro 2020 — controversy follows him like a loyal Labrador.

players.name: David Alaba
players.bio: Austria's greatest modern player, full stop. Alaba has won the Champions League with both Bayern Munich and Real Madrid, playing everywhere from left-back to centre-back. A true footballing polymath and national icon.
players.facts: Alaba has won the Champions League in two different decades with two different clubs. Collecting those like Panini stickers.

players.name: Marcel Sabitzer
players.bio: Sabitzer is Austria's Swiss Army knife — dangerous from distance, tireless in midfield, and a serial big-club player. With spells at RB Leipzig, Bayern, Man United, and now Dortmund, he's seen it all and scores memorable goals.
players.facts: Sabitzer's rocket against Germany at Euro 2020 is still replayed with reverence in Austrian living rooms nationwide.

players.name: Florian Grillitsch
players.bio: A deep-lying playmaker with a cultured left foot, Grillitsch reads the game like a veteran librarian. Now at Braga after stints in the Bundesliga, he brings composure and distribution to Austria's midfield base.
players.facts: Grillitsch became a free agent after Hoffenheim — a rare bargain nobody quite rushed to snap up at first.

players.name: Michael Gregoritsch
players.bio: A physically imposing striker who uses his frame brilliantly to hold up play, Gregoritsch has been a reliable scorer for Austria with 24 goals in 75 caps. Known for poaching important goals in big moments.
players.facts: Gregoritsch scored Austria's winner against Ukraine at Euro 2020 — a moment that ended a 31-year major tournament drought.

players.name: Florian Wiegele
players.bio: The third-choice keeper with just 1 cap, Wiegele is Austria's youngest goalkeeper in the squad. The Graz-born stopper has impressed at Viktoria Plzeň in the Czech top flight and is one for the future.
players.facts: Wiegele was born in 2001 — meaning he was still in primary school when David Alaba won his first Champions League.

players.name: Patrick Pentz
players.bio: Pentz is Austria's number two keeper, currently keeping clean sheets in Denmark with Brøndby. The Salzburg native is reliable with his feet and a commanding presence, waiting patiently in the wings behind Schlager.
players.facts: Pentz plays in Denmark — one of the few Austrian footballers whose club language barrier involves Danish pastry jokes.

players.name: Saša Kalajdžić
players.bio: A physical, direct centre-forward at hometown club LASK, Kalajdžić offers power and aerial threat as an impact option. With 22 caps and 4 international goals, he's a useful weapon off the bench at this World Cup.
players.facts: Kalajdžić's name features accented characters that have humbled many a foreign TV commentator — they respect him for it.

players.name: Philipp Lienhart
players.bio: A reliable and composed centre-back, Lienhart has been a consistent performer for SC Freiburg in the Bundesliga. With 41 caps and even 3 international goals to his name, he's a steady, underrated figure in Austria's defence.
players.facts: Lienhart was once on Real Madrid's books as a youth player — the Bernabéu path and the Freiburg path are very different rides.

players.name: Phillipp Mwene
players.bio: Born in Vienna to Zambian heritage, Mwene has been a dependable left-back option for Austria across 30 caps. His athleticism and defensive diligence have made him a trusted squad member at Mainz for years.
players.facts: Mwene's name is pronounced in ways that have baffled German Bundesliga commentators since approximately 2016.

players.name: Carney Chukwuemeka
players.bio: Born in Eisenstadt but raised in England, Chukwuemeka chose Austria over the Three Lions — a bold call that earned him 3 caps. The Dortmund midfielder is a box-to-box talent with big-game potential written all over him.
players.facts: Chukwuemeka played for England youth teams before switching to Austria — one of 2026's most intriguing national allegiance pivots.

players.name: Romano Schmid
players.bio: An energetic, creative attacking midfielder at Werder Bremen, Schmid brings flair and intensity to Austria's midfield. The Graz-born 26-year-old has grown steadily into a reliable international starter with 34 caps to his name.
players.facts: Schmid shares a club with Marco Friedl at Werder Bremen — the squad's unofficial Austrian colony in northern Germany.

players.name: Dejan Ljubičić
players.bio: A combative defensive midfielder who plays his club football at Schalke 04, Ljubičić brings physicality and energy to midfield battles. With 9 caps and a World Cup debut on the horizon, he's seizing his moment on the big stage.
players.facts: Ljubičić plays for Schalke 04 — a club so turbulent lately, surviving there is arguably better preparation than most cup runs.

players.name: Konrad Laimer
players.bio: One of Europe's finest right-backs, Laimer at Bayern Munich is all relentless energy and technical quality. His engine never seems to stop — he's the kind of player who probably tracks back in his sleep.
players.facts: Laimer joined Bayern Munich from RB Leipzig — essentially a Red Bull product graduating to... Red Bull's German rival. Cheers.

players.name: Patrick Wimmer
players.bio: A rapid and direct right winger at Wolfsburg, Wimmer loves to run at defenders and cause havoc in wide areas. Just 25 and already with 30 caps, he's one of Austria's most exciting attacking outlets at this tournament.
players.facts: Wimmer hails from Tulln — a town famous for painter Egon Schiele. Patrick's artistry is more in the final third than on canvas.

players.name: Alexander Prass
players.bio: A creative left-sided midfielder at Hoffenheim, Prass is one of Austria's emerging talents. Born in Hellmonsödt — a village of about 2,000 people — he's proof that big careers come from very small places.
players.facts: Prass grew up in Hellmonsödt, a village so small that his World Cup squad number probably exceeds its annual events calendar.

players.name: Marco Friedl
players.bio: A composed and technically solid left-back at Werder Bremen, Friedl has steadily built his international career to 11 caps. Reliable going forward and disciplined defensively, he's a trustworthy option in Austria's backline.
players.facts: Friedl and Romano Schmid are both Werder Bremen teammates — their car pool to Austria camp must be very efficient.

players.name: Paul Wanner
players.bio: Austria's teenage prodigy, Wanner made history as one of Bayern Munich's youngest ever first-team players before moving to PSV. Born in 2005, this is extraordinary — he's at a World Cup before most of us have had a proper job.
players.facts: Wanner was born in December 2005 — meaning he'll still be eligible for Austria's Under-21s after this World Cup. Ridiculous.

players.name: Michael Svoboda
players.bio: A tall, commanding centre-back who plies his trade with Venezia in Italy, Svoboda has shown international potential across 4 caps. Cool in possession with good aerial ability, he's Austria's emerging defensive option.
players.facts: Svoboda plays club football in Venice — presumably the only World Cup defender whose commute sometimes involves a gondola.

players.name: Alessandro Schöpf
players.bio: A versatile and experienced midfielder from the Tyrolean Alps, Schöpf has had stints at Schalke and Celta Vigo before returning to Austria with Wolfsberger AC. Six international goals show he's always had a goal threat in him.
players.facts: Schöpf is from Umhausen in the Ötz Valley — arguably the most dramatic mountain backdrop of any player at this World Cup.
